Task in computer vision involves keeping track of a specific target across a video sequence
Recent years have seen increased attention in computer vision towards using point clouds from 3D data acquisition devices for various vision tasks
Point clouds from 3D data acquisition devices are used for object detection
Point clouds from 3D data acquisition devices are used for object tracking
Point cloud-based object tracking shows huge potential in applications like autonomous driving
Existing 3D point cloud-based SOT methods are categorized into three paradigms, namely SC3D, P2B, and motion-centric
SC3D is a pioneering work that crops the target from the previous frame, while P2B takes the cropped target template to address efficiency problems

P2B reaches a balance between performance and speed by adopting a 3D region proposal network to predict the current bounding box. However, P2B overlooks the contextual information across two consecutive frames. The lack of contextual information may degrade tracking performance due to large appearance variation.
SC3D paradigm overlooks the contextual information across two consecutive frames. The lack of contextual information may cause appearance variation and drift towards intra-class distractors.
P2D paradigm overlooks the contextual information across two consecutive frames. The lack of contextual information may cause appearance variation and drift towards intra-class distractors.

M2-Track introduces a novel motion-centric paradigm that directly takes point clouds from two frames without cropping, segments target points from their surroundings, and estimates the current bounding box by explicitly modeling motion between the two frames. However, the motion-centric paradigm still works on cropped patches that lack contextual information in later localization.
The current bounding box may degrade tracking performance due to the existence of large appearance variation and widespread distractors.
CXTrack is a novel transformer-based tracker proposed to improve tracking performance specifically for 3D SOT.
CXTrack directly takes point clouds from two consecutive frames as input and specifies the target of interest with the previous bounding box to predict the current bounding box.
Local geometric information of the two point clouds is embedded into point features using a shared backbone network.
Targetness information is integrated into the point features based on the previous bounding box to propagate target cues into the current frame.
A target-centric transformer is used to propagate the target cues into the current frame while exploring contextual information in the surroundings of the target.
Enhanced point features are fed into a novel localization head named X-RPN to obtain the final target proposals.
X-RPN adopts a local transformer to model point feature interactions within the target, achieving a better balance between handling small and large objects compared with other localization heads.
The terms handling small and large objects and other localization heads are related in the context of comparing their effectiveness in distinguishing targets from distractors.
The novel center embedding module is incorporated into X-RPN to embed the relative target motion between frames and perform explicit motion modeling.
CXTrack significantly outperforms the current state-of-the-art methods in real-time tracking tasks on popular tracking datasets.
The new paradigm for the real-time 3D SOT task aims to exploit contextual information across consecutive frames to improve tracking accuracy.
CXTrack is a transformer-based tracker that utilizes a target-centric transformer architecture to propagate targetness information and exploit contextual details for tracking.
X-RPN is a localization head that is robust to intra-class distractors and balances between handling small and large targets effectively.
Early methods for the 3D SOT task focus on RGB-D information and tend to use 2D Siamese networks, but changes in illumination and appearance can impact the performance of these methods.
SC3D crops the target from the previous frame and computes the cosine similarity between targets, representing a pioneering work in the context of 3D object tracking.
The pipeline computes the cosine similarity between the target template and a series of 3D target proposals sampled from the current frame using a Siamese backbone.
The pipeline relies on heuristic sampling, which is very time-consuming.

P2B [20] develops an end-to-end framework, which first employs a shared backbone to embed local geometry into point features, and then propagates target cues from the target template to the search area in the current frame.
P2B [20] reaches a balance between performance and efficiency, and many works follow the same paradigm.
MLVSNet [29] aggregates information at multiple levels for more effective target localization.
BAT [31] introduces a box-aware feature fusion module to enhance the correlation learning between the target template and the search area.
V2B [8] proposes a voxel-to-BEV (Bird’s Eye View) target localization network, which projects the point features into a dense BEV feature map to tackle the sparsity of point clouds.
LTTR [3] adopts a transformer-based architecture to fuse features from two branches and propagate target cues.
PTT [22] integrates a transformer module into the P2B architecture to refine point features.
PTT integrates a transformer module into the P2B architecture to refine point features.
PTTR introduces Point Relation Transformer for feature fusion and a light-weight Prediction Refinement Module for coarse-to-fine localization.
PTTR introduces Point Relation Transformer for feature fusion and a light-weight Prediction Refinement Module for coarse-to-fine localization.
ST-Net develops an iterative coarse-to-fine correlation network for robust correlation learning.

Success and Precision are evaluation metrics used to measure the performance of a model based on AUC values under specific conditions.
Both Success and Precision metrics are defined based on predetermined thresholds for the Intersection Over Union and distance between centers, respectively.
Success metric denotes the Area Under Curve (AUC) for the plot displaying the ratio of frames satisfying a specific IOU threshold.
Precision is defined as the AUC for the plot showing the ratio of frames within a certain distance threshold.
Precision metric considers the distance between centers of predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes as per the given threshold values.
Success is calculated based on the IOU ratio between predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes exceeding a certain threshold limit.
Area Under Curve (AUC) is calculated from the plot depicting the relationship between predicted and ground-truth bounding box ratios.
Success metric is utilized for the evaluation of model performance under specific conditions.

STNet and M2-Track are compared in Tab. 1, where CXTrack surpasses previous state-of-the-art methods with a significant improvement of average Success and Precision. However, STNet surpasses CXTrack in the Car category by a minor margin (72.1/84.0 v.s. 69.1/81.6).
STNet uses voxel-based approach which performs well for vehicles with simple shapes and limited rotation angles, providing a strong shape prior for performance gain in large objects.
M2-Track shows a significant improvement (67.0/91.5 v.s. 49.9/77.2) in the Pedestrian category compared to previous methods.
CXTrack shows significant improvement on the Pedestrian category by handling distractors and better preserving contextual information.
CXTrack outperforms M2-Track in overall performance, especially in local geometry and contextual information.
CXTrack is compared to other methods for evaluating its generalization ability using KITTI pretrained model on nuScenes and WOD datasets.
M2-Track and CXTrack are both robust to distractors, but CXTrack makes more accurate predictions.

CXTrack's performance improvement is highlighted in the comparison with state of the arts on Waymo Open Dataset where it shows improvement in various metrics for different categories like Vehicle, Pedestrian, etc.
A comparison is made between Pedestrian and Cyclist categories in the nuScenes dataset.

CXTrack shows comparable performance on the nuScenes dataset and achieves a good balance between small and large objects. Pretrained models on nuScenes dataset are more challenging to generalize due to different LiDAR configurations.
CXTrack outperforms other methods in terms of average success and precision, notably on KITTI and WOD datasets captured by 64-beam LiDARs.
CXTrack achieves good accuracy on datasets captured with 64-beam LiDARs, showing a clear margin of success and precision.
CXTrack is more challenging to generalize to the nuScenes dataset captured by 32-beam LiDARs, but still achieves comparable performance.
CXTrack's performance is notable for its ability to generalize well even with differences in LiDAR configurations between datasets.
CXTrack achieves good accuracy in scenes with both sparse and dense point clouds, showing high performance and generalization abilities.

A new paradigm is proposed to fully exploit contextual information across frames for the 3D SOT task.
CXTrack is a novel transformer-based network designed to explore contextual information and model intra-frame and inter-frame feature relationships.
CXTrack includes a localization head named X-RPN to obtain high-quality proposals for objects of all sizes.
CXTrack incorporates a center embedding module to distinguish the target from distractors.
CXTrack significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-arts, showing its superiority.
CXTrack is robust to distractors, indicating its ability to handle distracting elements.
